text: Staff Training Module

text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments
textPreview: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the AI as a Public Health Systems Shift lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: AI changes public health infrastructure, workforce expectations, policy, data systems, procurement, public trust, communications, surveillance, and service delivery.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.

text: Guardrail
textPreview: Guardrail

text: Treating AI as a one-off technology purchase can create fragmented adoption, uneven safeguards, and unowned risk.

text:
Use this slide in the AI as a Public Health Systems Shift training module.
Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 2, 3, 9, 12 and Tools 4, 10, 6.
Emphasize the core guardrail: Treating AI as a one-off technology purchase can create fragmented adoption, uneven safeguards, and unowned risk.
Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.

textPreview: AI PLAYBOOK AND TOOLKIT

text: Module overview
textPreview: Module overview

text: AI adoption is a systems change for public health departments. It affects data modernization, procurement, workforce roles, governance authority, legal review, community engagement, communications, emergency response, program operations, and evaluation. Treating AI as a one-time technology purchase leaves agencies with fragmented pilots and unmanaged risk.

text: What this means in practice

text: Systems Affected
textPreview: Systems Affected

text: Data systems need quality checks, access rules, documentation, interoperability, and appropriate AI environments. Workforce systems need training, role clarity, bridge professionals, change support, and leadership expectations. Governance.

text: Planning Implications
textPreview: Planning Implications

text: Sequence adoption through plays rather than approving isolated pilots. Connect AI planning to data modernization, workforce development, funding, procurement, and quality improvement. Identify where existing policies can be extended and where.
textPreview: Sequence adoption through plays rather than approving isolated pilots. Connect AI planning to data modernization, workforce development, ...

text: Leadership Role
textPreview: Leadership Role

text: Leaders should set the expectation that responsible AI is mission infrastructure, not a side project. Executive sponsors should ensure governance has authority, staff have time, and public accountability is built into decisions.

text:
Use this slide in the AI as a Public Health Systems Shift training module.
Use this slide to make the topic concrete through public health workflow examples.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 2, 3, 9, 12 and Tools 4, 10, 6.
For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?

text: Training exercise
textPreview: Training exercise

text: Create a systems map showing how AI adoption affects governance, data, technology, workforce, procurement, communications, equity, and funding. Identify gaps that must be addressed before pilots scale.

text: AI as a Public Health Systems Shift
textPreview: AI as a Public Health Systems Shift

text:
Use this slide in the AI as a Public Health Systems Shift training module.
Use this slide for a facilitated activity near the end of the lesson.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 2, 3, 9, 12 and Tools 4, 10, 6.
Give participants time to work individually or in small groups. Ask them to produce a practical artifact that can feed into readiness assessment, governance review, use case prioritization, or implementation planning.
Expected takeaways include: AI systems-change map, Cross-program implementation dependencies, Leadership decision agenda.
